title: Virtual Environments
content:
### Virtual Environments

A **virtual environment** is an isolated Python environment for a specific project.

---

### Why Use Virtual Environments?

- **Isolation** -- project A can use Flask 2.x, project B uses Flask 1.x
- **No conflicts** -- prevents dependency clashes
- **Clean dependencies** -- only install what your project needs
- **Reproducibility** -- share `requirements.txt` with team

---

### Creating a Virtual Environment

```bash
# Create environment
python -m venv myenv

# Activate (Windows)
myenv\Scripts\activate

# Activate (Mac/Linux)
source myenv/bin/activate
```

---

### Working in an Environment

```bash
# After activation, prompt changes to (myenv)
(myenv) $ pip install flask requests
(myenv) $ python myapp.py

# Deactivate when done
(myenv) $ deactivate
```

---

### Workflow

```bash
# Create project directory
mkdir myproject
cd myproject

# Create virtual environment
python -m venv .venv

# Activate it
.venv\Scripts\activate  # Windows
source .venv/bin/activate  # Mac/Linux

# Install packages
pip install flask

# Save dependencies
pip freeze > requirements.txt
```

---

### .gitignore

Always add `.venv/` to `.gitignore` -- do not commit your virtual environment.

```
# .gitignore
.venv/
__pycache__/
*.pyc
```

---

> **Best Practice:** Always use a virtual environment for every Python project. It is the standard practice for professional Python development.
codeExample:
# Virtual environment commands (run in terminal, not Python)

'''
# Create
python -m venv myenv

# Activate (Windows)
myenv\Scripts\activate

# Activate (Mac/Linux)
source myenv/bin/activate

# Install
pip install requests

# Save dependencies
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# Exit
deactivate
'''
